target: data/wins.json WIN-068 detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text — major rewrite for Self-Contradicted verdict (Refraction Cascade)
kernel_of_truth: The dome's ensemble approach is methodologically sound in principle — aggregating data across 39 eclipses shows statistical awareness. Kim & Chang (2018) is genuine peer-reviewed science confirming real eclipse-induced geomagnetic effects. The dome also correctly identifies that eclipses produce measurable magnetic variations, and its 2026 eclipse predictions (E001-E009) register specific quantitative targets — more predictive commitment than most alternative models ever attempt.
holes_addressed: CRITICAL: verdict badge (Self-Contradicted) disagreed with detail text (Std Model Explains argument only)
MAJOR: No mention of refraction cascade or sun altitude indeterminacy in WIN-068 text
MAJOR: TLDRs described wrong verdict (already fixed by decider)
MODERATE: detail_verdict_text led with post-hoc boilerplate instead of self-contradiction
MODERATE: No preemptive text against apparent-vs-physical shadow defense
new_evidence_added: Refraction cascade argument: dome's apparent/physical sun altitude split makes physical sun position seasonally indeterminate (1,975-3,618 km)
FSF connection: dome's own 2026 eclipse predictions (E001-E009) use delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF, where FSF derives from H(r) — the same function whose indeterminacy the cascade documents

Preemptive defense neutralization: dome mechanism is physical field blockage ('aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow'), not photoionization — so it requires physical, not apparent, sun position

anticipated_objections.objection: The eclipse magnetic prediction tracks observed/apparent shadow geometry, not physical sun position. The Sq current mechanism depends on photoionization reduction which follows the apparent shadow path. The refraction cascade is irrelevant because the magnetically-relevant shadow is the one observers see.
anticipated_objections.response: This defense works for the standard (Chapman) mechanism, which tracks apparent shadow coverage. But the dome's claimed mechanism is not photoionization — it is 'aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow blocking the field interaction between sun and dome' (raw-text/02-wins.txt). Physical field blockage requires physical positions. If the dome retreats to 'the effect tracks apparent positions,' it is conceding the Chapman mechanism while wearing dome vocabulary — which is the 'Std Model Explains' argument. The self-contradiction argument is model-specific: it attacks what the dome says its mechanism is.

anticipated_objections.objection: The cascade only affects the physical sun altitude, but the 39-eclipse ensemble is about ground-level magnetic measurements which don't depend on sun altitude.
anticipated_objections.response: Ground-level magnetic measurements depend on the eclipse shadow geometry — specifically, where the shadow falls on the ionosphere and how much area it covers. In the dome model, shadow geometry is determined by the relative positions of the sun and moon. If the dome's physical sun altitude is seasonally indeterminate (1,975-3,618 km), the shadow's path across the disc for each eclipse becomes indeterminate. The dome cannot specify which ionospheric regions were shaded during any particular eclipse without knowing where the sun physically was.

anticipated_objections.objection: WIN-010 is also an eclipse magnetic WIN and is in the cascade table as 'supplementary, no verdict change.' Why does WIN-068 get Self-Contradicted but WIN-010 doesn't?
anticipated_objections.response: Section 1.8 explains: WIN-010's argument is about magnetic anomaly magnitude via the Chapman ionospheric mechanism — a mechanistically distinct argument unaffected by the physical altitude swing. WIN-068 is the ensemble claim covering 39 eclipses across different geometries, seasons, and latitudes. The cascade's force is proportional to the geometric diversity of the dataset — across 39 eclipses spanning 25 years and varying seasonal sun positions, the dome would need to specify 39 different physical sun altitudes it cannot compute.

replacement_fields.detail_evidence:
The underlying data is real and well-documented. Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054) conducted a rigorous statistical analysis of geomagnetic field variations during 39 solar eclipses using INTERMAGNET data. They confirmed systematic patterns: Y-component increases, X/Z/F decreases, strongest at magnetic latitudes 30°–50°, over ~180-minute windows centered on maximum eclipse. This is genuine, peer-reviewed science.

Self-contradiction: the dome’s own refraction fix breaks this prediction. Section 1.8 documents how the dome’s aetheric refraction correction — introduced to resolve the sun/firmament collision at 5,733 km — splits the sun’s altitude into an "apparent" value (5,733 km, an optical illusion) and a "physical" value that must stay below the firmament ceiling H(r). But H(r) varies with orbital radius: ~3,618 km at the Tropic of Cancer and ~1,975 km at the Tropic of Capricorn. The physical sun altitude therefore oscillates by over 1,600 km across seasons — a swing the dome has never modelled. Eclipse shadow geometry requires a definite physical sun position to determine where the shadow falls and how it moves. Across 39 eclipses spanning 25 years and all seasons, the dome cannot specify the physical sun altitude for any of them without a seasonal model it does not have. This makes the ensemble prediction indeterminate: the dome cannot retrodict the shadow paths it claims to explain.

This is not an abstract concern. The dome’s own 2026 eclipse predictions (E001–E009) use the formula ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF, where the Field Strength Factor (FSF) derives from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry — the same function whose seasonal indeterminacy Section 1.8 documents. The dome’s future quantitative predictions are built on the function that makes its past claims indeterminate.

The dome’s claimed mechanism is "aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow blocking the field interaction between sun and dome" (raw-text/02-wins.txt). This is a physical field interaction: the shadow physically blocks an energy pathway. Unlike the standard photoionization mechanism (which tracks the apparent shadow path as seen from the ionosphere), a physical field blockage requires the physical sun position to determine shadow geometry. The refraction cascade directly undermines this: if the physical sun position is seasonally indeterminate, so is the physical shadow that supposedly blocks the aetheric field.

Independent of the self-contradiction, the mechanism has been well understood since Chapman (1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3). During a solar eclipse, the Moon’s shadow reduces photoionization in the E-region ionosphere (90–150 km altitude), decreasing electron density and electrical conductivity. This weakens the solar-quiet (Sq) dynamo current system. The Sq conductivity framework (Yamazaki & Maute 2021) predicts the qualitative pattern — Y-component increase, X/Z/F decrease, peak at eclipse maximum, strongest at Sq-vortex latitudes (~30°–50°) — from measured ionospheric electron content depletion. The dome provides no derivation showing how its cavity geometry produces the specific component patterns, the latitude dependence, or the 180-minute timescale. The standard model derives all three from the geometry of the Sq current vortex.

The dome’s Steelman Evidence Ladder places eclipse geomagnetic evidence at Tier 1, explicitly acknowledging it is consistent with both models — an admission that WIN-068 alone cannot distinguish dome cavity dynamics from standard ionospheric physics. This WIN also duplicates the observational basis of WIN-010 (BOU 2017 eclipse, −10.9 nT) and WIN-025 (2024 9-station confirmation). All three cite the same physical phenomenon at different aggregation levels: single-station, 9-station, and 39-eclipse ensemble. Counting the same observation three times inflates the WIN count.
replacement_fields.detail_verdict_text:
This claim is self-contradicted by the dome’s own architecture. The refraction fix documented in Section 1.8 makes the physical sun altitude seasonally indeterminate (1,975–3,618 km), and eclipse shadow geometry requires a definite physical sun position. Across 39 eclipses spanning different seasons and geometries, the dome cannot specify where the physical sun was for any of them. The dome’s own 2026 eclipse predictions (E001–E009) compound this by explicitly using H(r)-derived FSF values — doubling down on the function whose seasonal indeterminacy the cascade exposes.

The eclipse geomagnetic effect has additionally been explained by ionospheric conductivity reduction (the Chapman Sq mechanism) since 1933 — 93 years before the dome model claimed it. Kim & Chang (2018) is real peer-reviewed science, but it confirms standard ionospheric physics, not dome cavity dynamics. The dome concedes this is non-discriminating at Tier 1.

Independent of the scientific critique, this claim also fails the post-hoc test. The confirming data comes from Kim & Chang’s published dataset covering 1991–2016, publicly available well before the dome incorporated it. Under the dome author’s own timestamping definition, no pre-registration of the expected value in a venue independent of the author could be located. The monitor script has no validation logic for WIN-068 (monitoring = "none"), and the 39-eclipse ensemble is a retrospective literature citation, not a live prediction.

cross_win_note: WIN-025 (same detail_group, same phenomenon) remains 'Std Model Explains' and is NOT in the Section 1.8 cascade table. The curmudgeon flags this as an exploitable inconsistency. This is out of scope for EXP-101 (which covers WIN-068 only) but should be tracked as a separate issue. WIN-025's status is also pending human decision (ISS-789).
